items.subject: ecology
items.topic: how invasive species disrupt native food webs
items.transcript: When a species shows up somewhere it has never lived before, the consequences can ripple far beyond that single newcomer. This is the heart of invasion ecology, the study of how non-native species reshape the food webs they enter. A food web, remember, is just the network of who eats whom in an ecosystem. Introduce one new node into that network and the connections can rearrange in surprising ways. Consider the brown tree snake, which arrived on the Pacific island of Guam sometime after the Second World War, probably as a stowaway in cargo. Guam's forest birds had evolved with no snake predators at all, so they had no defenses, no instinct to avoid it. Within a few decades the snake had eaten its way through nearly the entire native bird population. Now, here is the part students often miss. Those birds weren't just prey. Many of them pollinated flowers and dispersed seeds. With the birds gone, certain trees stopped reproducing, and the spiders the birds had once kept in check multiplied enormously. So the snake didn't simply remove one link. It pulled apart connections several steps away from itself. This is why ecologists worry so much about invasions. The damage isn't proportional to the invader's size or its appetite. A single species can act like a loose thread that unravels an entire fabric, because food webs depend on relationships that took thousands of years to settle into balance. Repairing them, if it is even possible, can take far longer.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How an introduced species can unravel a food web
options.B: The ways forest birds pollinate flowers and spread tree seeds
options.C: Methods scientists use to remove snakes from islands
options.D: How cargo ships accidentally transport animals across oceans

questions.explanation: 讲座核心是入侵物种如何打乱整个食物网，以棕树蛇为例说明影响远超单一捕食关系，故A正确。B只是讲座中提到的一个细节（鸟的生态作用），范围太窄，属于too_narrow。C讨论清除蛇的方法，讲座从未涉及，属于wrong_topic。D抓住'stowaway in cargo'这个表层词，但货运并非主旨，属于surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention that Guam's birds had 'no instinct to avoid it'?

options.A: To suggest the birds were less intelligent than mainland species
options.B: To explain why the snake devastated the bird population so quickly
options.C: To describe the birds' role in pollinating the island's flowers
options.D: To prove that all island animals lack natural defenses

questions.explanation: 教授指出鸟类与蛇共同进化经历为零，因而毫无防御本能，这解释了蛇为何能在几十年内几乎吃光鸟群，故B正确。A把缺乏本能误读为'不够聪明'，曲解了目的，属于wrong_purpose。C描述的是鸟的传粉作用，是另一处内容，并非提及此句的原因，属于literal_restatement/内容混淆（wrong_purpose）。D将特例夸大为'所有岛屿动物'，强加了教授未表达的论断，属于over_inference。

questions.stem: Why does the professor compare the snake to 'a loose thread'?

options.A: To restate that the snake ate nearly all of Guam's native birds
options.B: To argue that snakes are physically weaker than most people assume
options.C: To emphasize that one species can cause damage far beyond itself
options.D: To recommend a specific strategy for controlling invasions

questions.explanation: '松线扯散整块布'的比喻强调单一物种的破坏会沿着食物网蔓延、远超其自身，故C正确。A只是复述蛇吃光了鸟这一事实，没有解释比喻的目的，属于literal_restatement。B谈蛇的体力强弱，曲解了比喻的意图，属于wrong_purpose。D说在推荐防控策略，讲座并未给出策略，属于over_inference。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the harm caused by invasive species?

options.A: It is always reversed once the invader is removed
options.B: It is limited to the species the invader directly eats
options.C: It is easy to predict from the invader's body size
options.D: It can be disproportionate to the invader's own characteristics

questions.explanation: 教授说'破坏与入侵者的体型或食量不成比例'，暗示危害可能远大于其自身特征，故D正确。A称移除入侵者后破坏总能恢复，但教授说修复'即便可能也要更久'，与原意相反，属于reversed_logic。B将危害局限于直接被吃的物种，但讲座强调连锁影响波及数步之外，属于reversed_logic。C说危害可由体型轻松预测，恰与'不成比例'相悖，属于topic_adjacent/反向。

items.subject: history
items.topic: the unexpected origins of the modern fire department
items.transcript: The publicly funded fire department, the one you call by dialing a number and that costs you nothing at the moment of crisis, is a fairly recent invention. For most of history, fighting fires was a private business, and a rather cynical one. In ancient Rome, a wealthy man named Crassus ran what amounted to a fire brigade for profit. When a building caught fire, his crew would rush to the scene and then simply stand there, refusing to act until the owner agreed to sell the burning property at a steep discount. Only after the deal was struck would the firefighters go to work. That model survived in various forms for centuries. By the seventeen hundreds, insurance companies in London had their own brigades. Each insured building displayed a metal badge, called a fire mark, near its door. If a brigade arrived and saw a rival company's mark, or no mark at all, they might leave the building to burn. You can imagine the problems. Fires don't respect property lines, so a blaze ignored by one company could spread to buildings the same company did insure. Eventually the insurers realized that cooperating, and protecting whole neighborhoods, served everyone's interest better than competing. Those private brigades gradually merged, and cities began taking the service over and funding it through taxes. So the fire department we treat as an obvious public good actually grew out of profit schemes and competing insurance companies. It became public not because someone designed it that way from the start, but because the private alternatives kept failing in ways nobody could ignore.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How modern insurance companies calculate the total cost of fire damage
options.B: How the public fire department evolved from private, profit-driven systems
options.C: The engineering advances that made early firefighting equipment more effective
options.D: Why ancient Rome experienced so many destructive building fires

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是公共消防如何从以盈利为目的的私人体系演变而来，故B正确。A谈保险公司如何计算损失，讲座未涉及，属于wrong_topic。C讲消防设备的工程进步，讲座完全没有提及，属于wrong_topic。D抓住'罗马'与'火灾'的表层词，但罗马火灾频发并非主旨，属于surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did Crassus's fire brigade do upon arriving at a fire?

options.A: They immediately extinguished the flames at no charge
options.B: They looked for a metal fire mark mounted beside the building's door
options.C: They waited until the owner agreed to sell the property cheaply
options.D: They evacuated nearby neighbors before fighting the blaze

questions.explanation: 讲座明确说克拉苏的队伍到场后按兵不动，直到业主同意低价出售房产才动手，故C正确。A说他们免费立即灭火，与盈利模式相反，属于reversed_logic。B把火灾标记（fire mark）这一伦敦保险公司的做法张冠李戴到罗马，属于wrong_detail。D说先疏散邻居，讲座从未提及，属于topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why did London's insurance brigades eventually cooperate?

options.A: A new law forced all brigades to merge into one service
options.B: Fire marks became too expensive for buildings to display
options.C: The government offered the companies large cash rewards
options.D: They realized protecting whole neighborhoods served everyone better

questions.explanation: 讲座说保险商最终意识到合作、保护整个街区比互相竞争对所有人都更有利，故D正确。A称有新法律强制合并，讲座说的是逐渐自发合并，属于wrong_detail。B说火灾标记太贵，讲座未提及成本问题，属于topic_adjacent。C说政府提供巨额现金奖励，讲座从未提及，属于topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about why firefighting became a public service?

options.A: The private systems repeatedly failed in ways that could not be ignored
options.B: A single visionary planned the public system from the very beginning
options.C: Public fire departments have always existed throughout human history
options.D: Insurance companies were legally banned from fighting any fires

questions.explanation: 教授结尾指出它变为公共服务不是因为有人一开始就设计好，而是因为私人方案不断以无法忽视的方式失败，故A正确。B说有一位远见者从一开始就规划好，恰与'不是设计出来的'相反，属于reversed_logic。C说公共消防一直存在，与'相当晚近的发明'矛盾，属于reversed_logic。D说保险公司被法律禁止灭火，讲座未提及，属于topic_adjacent。

items.subject: social_psychology
items.topic: the diffusion of responsibility in large groups
items.transcript: Imagine someone collapses on a crowded sidewalk. You might assume that the more people who are around, the faster that person gets help. It turns out the opposite is often true, and social psychologists have a name for why: the diffusion of responsibility. The idea is simple. When many people witness the same emergency, each person feels only a small share of the responsibility to act. Everyone assumes someone else will step in, so often no one does. Now, the classic study here goes back to the nineteen sixties. Researchers placed individual volunteers in a room and piped in sounds suggesting another person nearby was having a medical crisis. Some volunteers believed they were the only witness. Others believed several people could also hear it. The result was striking. When volunteers thought they alone could help, almost all of them responded quickly. But when they believed others were present, far fewer acted, and those who did took much longer to react. Notice what this tells us. The hesitation wasn't about cruelty or not caring. The same person who would rush to help when alone might freeze in a crowd. Responsibility, in a sense, gets divided up among everyone present until each individual share feels too small to act on. This is why emergency trainers now teach you to point at one specific person and say, you, call for help. Naming a single individual cancels the diffusion, because that one person can no longer assume the job belongs to somebody else.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How medical crises are diagnosed by trained emergency workers
options.B: Why some individuals are naturally crueler than others
options.C: Why people are often less likely to help in a crowd
options.D: How researchers in the nineteen sixties designed soundproof rooms

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是责任分散：人多时每个人反而更不愿出手相助，故C正确。A谈医疗诊断，讲座未涉及，属于wrong_topic。B说有些人天生更冷酷，讲座明确否认这与冷酷有关，属于wrong_topic/反向。D抓住'房间'与'六十年代'的表层词，但隔音房间设计并非主旨，属于surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did the nineteen-sixties study find?

options.A: Volunteers who were in groups responded much faster than those left alone
options.B: All volunteers refused to help regardless of the situation
options.C: Volunteers helped only when they were paid to participate
options.D: Volunteers who thought they were alone almost always helped quickly

questions.explanation: 讲座说当志愿者认为只有自己能帮忙时，几乎都会迅速反应，故D正确。A说人多时反应更快，与研究结果相反，属于reversed_logic。B说所有人都拒绝帮忙，讲座说独处者几乎都帮，属于wrong_detail。C说只有付钱才帮，讲座从未提及报酬，属于topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe pointing at one person and saying 'you, call for help'?

options.A: To show how assigning responsibility to one person overcomes diffusion
options.B: To restate that emergencies happen on crowded sidewalks
options.C: To argue that shouting is the most effective way to treat injuries
options.D: To suggest that bystanders are usually unwilling to follow instructions

questions.explanation: 教授举这个例子是为了说明点名指定一个人能取消责任分散，使其无法再假设别人会做，故A正确。B只是复述紧急情况发生在拥挤人行道上，没解释举例目的，属于literal_restatement。C说喊叫是治疗伤者的最佳方式，曲解了目的，属于wrong_purpose。D说旁观者通常不愿服从指令，强加了教授未表达的论断，属于over_inference。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about a person who fails to help in a crowd?

options.A: That person is permanently incapable of helping anyone
options.B: That same person might well have helped if alone
options.C: That person enjoys watching others suffer in emergencies
options.D: That person did not actually notice the emergency at all

questions.explanation: 教授说同一个人独处时会冲上去帮忙，在人群中却可能僵住，暗示其在独处时很可能会施救，故B正确。A说该人永远无法帮任何人，过度夸大，属于over_generalization。C说该人乐于看别人受苦，与'并非冷酷'相反，属于reversed_logic。D说该人根本没注意到紧急情况，讲座说他们听到了却犹豫，属于topic_adjacent。

items.subject: astronomy
items.topic: the role of dark matter in galaxy formation
items.transcript: If you add up all the stars, gas, and dust we can actually see in a galaxy, you run into a problem. There simply isn't enough visible matter to explain how galaxies hold together or how they formed in the first place. The leading solution is dark matter, a kind of matter that gives off no light at all, yet makes up most of the mass in the universe. We can't see it directly, but we can detect its gravity. Here's the key piece of evidence. When astronomers measure how fast stars orbit the center of a galaxy, those at the outer edges move much faster than the visible matter alone could explain. If only the stars and gas we see were present, the outer stars should slow down with distance, like planets far from the sun. Instead they keep racing along, which tells us there must be a vast, invisible halo of mass surrounding the galaxy. Dark matter also shaped galaxies from the beginning. In the early universe, dark matter clumped together first, forming a kind of gravitational scaffolding. Ordinary gas then fell into those clumps, cooled, and ignited into stars. So in a sense, the galaxies we admire are built on an invisible framework we still don't fully understand. What strikes me most is that the matter making up you, me, and every star we can name is the rare exception. The cosmos is dominated by something we have never directly seen.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How telescopes capture light from the most distant stars
options.B: Why planets far from the sun orbit more slowly than near ones
options.C: The chemical process by which gas cools into a single star
options.D: The role dark matter plays in holding and forming galaxies

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是暗物质在维系和形成星系中的作用，故D正确。A谈望远镜捕捉星光，讲座未涉及，属于wrong_topic。B只是讲座用来做对比的一个细节（远行星转得慢），并非主旨，属于too_narrow。C讲气体冷却成单颗恒星的化学过程，是局部细节且被放大，属于too_narrow。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what do the outer stars of a galaxy do?

options.A: They move faster than the visible matter alone could explain
options.B: They give off more light than stars near the galactic center
options.C: They gradually fall inward toward the center of the galaxy
options.D: They slow down with distance, just as planets do from the sun

questions.explanation: 讲座说外缘恒星运动速度远超仅靠可见物质所能解释的程度，故A正确。B说外缘恒星比中心恒星发更多光，讲座未提及亮度对比，属于topic_adjacent。C说它们逐渐向内坠落，讲座未提及，属于topic_adjacent。D说它们随距离变慢像行星一样，这正是若无暗物质'本应'发生但实际没发生的情况，属于reversed_logic。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what happened during the early formation of galaxies?

options.A: Ordinary gas clumped together before any dark matter did
options.B: Dark matter clumped first and formed a gravitational scaffolding
options.C: Stars formed first and then attracted dark matter around them
options.D: Visible light pushed gas outward to the edges of each galaxy

questions.explanation: 讲座说在早期宇宙中暗物质先聚集，形成一种引力'脚手架'，普通气体随后落入，故B正确。A说普通气体先于暗物质聚集，顺序颠倒，属于reversed_logic。C说恒星先形成再吸引暗物质，同样把顺序弄反，属于reversed_logic。D说可见光把气体推向边缘，讲座从未提及，属于topic_adjacent/wrong_detail。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about ordinary, visible matter in the universe?

options.A: It is gradually being converted into dark matter over time
options.B: It emits the gravity that astronomers use to map galaxies
options.C: It makes up only a small fraction of the universe's mass
options.D: It is evenly spread across every region of the cosmos

questions.explanation: 教授结尾说构成我们和恒星的物质是'罕见的例外'，宇宙由从未直接见过的东西主导，暗示可见物质只占宇宙质量的一小部分，故C正确。A说可见物质正逐渐变成暗物质，讲座未提及任何转化，属于topic_adjacent。B说可见物质发出用于绘制星系的引力，但讲座说探测的是暗物质的引力，属于reversed_logic。D说可见物质均匀分布于宇宙各处，讲座说它聚集在暗物质团块中，属于over_generalization。
